deep_dive.title: Federated Cross-Client Subgraph Pattern Detection
deep_dive.abstract: Subgraph pattern detection aims to uncover complex interaction structures in graphs. However, state-of-the-art graph neural network (GNN)-based solutions assume centralized access to the entire graph. When graphs are instead distributed across multiple parties, client-local GNN computations diverge from those of a centralized model, resulting in a representation-equivalence gap. We formalize this as a structural observability problem, where subgraph patterns crossing partition boundaries become locally unidentifiable. To bridge this gap, we propose a per-step, layer-wise embedding exchange framework in which clients synchronize intermediate node representations at each layer of the forward pass, without exposing raw features or labels. Under an extended-subgraph assumption and shared model parameters across clients, this framework recovers the same node representations as a centralized GNN over the full graph. Experiments on synthetic directed multigraphs with cycles, bicliques, and scatter-gather patterns show that embedding exchange and federated parameter aggregation are complementary rather than interchangeable: their combination recovers most of the representation gap, provided exchanged embeddings are fresh per-step rather than stale per-epoch.
deep_dive.llm_evidence_en: Federated GNN with layer-wise embedding exchange across graph partitions
deep_dive.llm_evidence_cn: 跨图分区的联邦GNN逐层嵌入交换
deep_dive.llm_evidence: 跨图分区的联邦GNN逐层嵌入交换
deep_dive.canonical_evidence: 跨图分区的联邦GNN逐层嵌入交换
deep_dive.llm_tldr_en: Proposes a federated GNN framework that exchanges intermediate embeddings across partitioned graphs to detect cross-client subgraph patterns.
deep_dive.llm_tldr_cn: 提出联邦GNN框架，通过交换中间嵌入检测跨客户端子图模式。
deep_dive.llm_tldr: 提出联邦GNN框架，通过交换中间嵌入检测跨客户端子图模式。
deep_dive.matched_query_text: graph partitioning strategies for distributed GNN

quick_skim.title: From Regression to Inference: Meta-Learning Predictors for Neural Architecture Search
quick_skim.abstract: Prediction-based approaches are widely used in neural architecture search (NAS), where a predictor estimates the performance of candidate architectures to guide selection. However, existing predictors are typically trained via supervised regression on limited samples, leading to overfitting and poor generalization to unseen architectures. In this work, we propose a fundamentally different formulation that models performance prediction as a conditional function inference problem using a Convolutional Neural Process (ConvNP) with meta-learning capabilities. Instead of fitting a fixed mapping to limited samples, our approach meta-learns to infer performance from partial observations by training with context-target splits across a group of synthesized tasks, explicitly optimizing for generalization under data scarcity and aligning the training procedure with the deployment setting in NAS. We further design simple yet effective meta-features for cell-based architectures and evaluate our method on NAS-Bench-101 and NAS-Bench-201. Extensive experiments show that our approach consistently improves top-K ranking quality and achieves the state-of-the-art architecture selection using limited samples.
quick_skim.llm_evidence_en: neural architecture search with meta-learning performance predictors
quick_skim.llm_evidence_cn: 基于元学习性能预测器的神经架构搜索
quick_skim.llm_evidence: 基于元学习性能预测器的神经架构搜索
quick_skim.canonical_evidence: 基于元学习性能预测器的神经架构搜索
quick_skim.llm_tldr_en: Proposes ConvNP-based meta-learning predictor for NAS that generalizes better than supervised regression approaches.
quick_skim.llm_tldr_cn: 提出基于ConvNP的元学习NAS预测器，比监督回归方法泛化能力更强。
quick_skim.llm_tldr: 提出基于ConvNP的元学习NAS预测器，比监督回归方法泛化能力更强。

quick_skim.title: FedQueue: Queue-Aware Federated Learning for Cross-Facility HPC Training
quick_skim.abstract: Federated learning (FL) across multiple HPC facilities faces stochastic admission delays from batch schedulers that dominate wall-clock time. Synchronous FL suffers from severe stragglers, while asynchronous FL accumulates stale updates when queues spike. We propose FedQueue, a queue-aware FL protocol that incorporates scheduler delays directly into training and aggregation, which (i) predicts per-facility queue delays online to budget local work, (ii) applies cutoff-based admission that buffers late arrivals to bound staleness, and (iii) performs staleness-aware aggregation to stabilize heterogeneous local workloads. We prove the convergence for non-convex objectives at rate $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{R})$ under bounded staleness, and show that the admission controls yield bounded staleness with high probability under queue-prediction error. Real-world cross-facility deployment of FedQueue shows 20.5% improvement over baseline algorithms. Controlled queue simulations demonstrate robust improvement over the baselines; in particular, about 34% reduction in time to reach a target accuracy level under high queue variance and non-IID partitions.
quick_skim.llm_evidence_en: Asynchronous distributed training with staleness-aware aggregation
quick_skim.llm_evidence_cn: 带有延迟感知聚合的异步分布式训练
quick_skim.llm_evidence: 带有延迟感知聚合的异步分布式训练
quick_skim.canonical_evidence: 带有延迟感知聚合的异步分布式训练
quick_skim.llm_tldr_en: FedQueue proposes a queue-aware federated learning protocol to handle stragglers and stale updates in asynchronous distributed training.
quick_skim.llm_tldr_cn: FedQueue提出队列感知联邦学习协议，解决异步分布式训练中的掉队者和延迟更新问题。

quick_skim.title: ATLAS: Efficient Out-of-Core Inference for Billion-Scale Graph Neural Networks
quick_skim.abstract: Graph Neural Network (GNN) inference on billion-scale graphs is critical for domains like fintech and recommendation systems. Full-graph inference on these large graphs can be challenging due to high communication costs in distributed settings and high I/O costs in disk-backed Out-of-Core (OOC) settings. Existing OOC systems, operating across disk and memory, primarily focus on GNN training and perform poorly for full-graph inference due to massive read amplification, irregular I/O, and memory pressure. We present ATLAS, a disk-based GNN inference framework that enables efficient full-graph, layer-wise inference on graphs whose topologies, features and intermediate embeddings exceed the available memory on single machines. ATLAS replaces gather-based execution with a broadcast-based model that enables sequential, single-pass streaming reads of features and embeddings per layer. A tiered memory-disk hierarchy with minimum-pending-message eviction, graph reordering and a GPU-accelerated pipeline sustains high throughput within $128$ GiB RAM and $2$ TiB SSD. Across out-of-core graphs with up to $4$B edges and $550$ GiB features and multiple GNN architectures, ATLAS improves end-to-end inference time by $\approx12$--$30\times$ over State-of-the-Art (SOTA) OOC baselines on a single workstation, while remaining within $\approx5\%$ when features fit in memory.
quick_skim.llm_evidence_en: efficient large-scale GNN system addressing communication costs
quick_skim.llm_evidence_cn: 解决通信成本的高效大规模GNN系统
quick_skim.llm_evidence: 解决通信成本的高效大规模GNN系统
quick_skim.canonical_evidence: 解决通信成本的高效大规模GNN系统
quick_skim.llm_tldr_en: ATLAS is a disk-based GNN inference framework that reduces I/O and communication costs for billion-scale graphs.
quick_skim.llm_tldr_cn: ATLAS是降低十亿级图I/O和通信成本的基于磁盘的GNN推理框架。
quick_skim.llm_tldr: ATLAS是降低十亿级图I/O和通信成本的基于磁盘的GNN推理框架。
